intent: Check whether the candidate can accurately walk through the NestJS request-response lifecycle and place middleware, guards, interceptors, pipes, handlers, services, and filters in the correct order.
primaryQuestion: What is the full execution order in NestJS from request in to response out?

expectedSignals.label: Gives the inbound order: incoming request, middleware, guards, interceptors before handler, pipes, controller handler, and service if the handler delegates to one.

requirements.description: Lists the inbound order from request through middleware, guards, pre-handler interceptors, pipes, controller handler, and delegated service.
Specifically places pipes after the interceptor pre-handler step and before the controller method receives arguments.

expectedSignals.label: Gives the outbound order: route-level interceptor response path, then controller-level interceptor, then global interceptor, then server response.

requirements.description: Explains the response path unwinds through route, controller, then global interceptors.
Connects outbound interceptor order to response mapping, logging, or envelope behavior before the server sends the response.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions binding scope order for most components: global, controller, route; middleware has global and module-bound ordering.

requirements.description: Mentions global, controller, and route/method binding scopes for Nest components.
Distinguishes middleware ordering through global app.use and module-bound MiddlewareConsumer registration.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that exception filters are invoked only for uncaught exceptions and resolve from the lowest matching scope to controller and then global.

requirements.description: States that exception filters participate when an exception is thrown or remains uncaught.
Explains that matching filters resolve from the closest route/method scope outward to controller and global scope.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that an uncaught exception short-circuits the normal path and jumps to the exception filter layer.

requirements.description: Explains that an uncaught exception skips the normal success response path.
States that the exception is handled by the appropriate exception filter layer.

expectedSignals.label: Can map real concerns to the right phase, such as authz in guards, DTO validation in pipes, and response mapping in interceptors.

requirements.description: Places authorization or route access decisions in guards.
Places DTO validation in pipes and response mapping/envelopes in interceptors.

scoringHints.description: Provides a precise ordered lifecycle, includes scope order, error short-circuiting, and explains why each component belongs in that phase.
Gets the main sequence right but misses one nuance such as interceptor return order or filter scope order.
Knows the component names but cannot produce a reliable request-to-response sequence.
Answer is generic Express-style middleware discussion without NestJS lifecycle detail.

followUps.question: If a route has a global guard, controller guard, route guard, route interceptor, and ValidationPipe, which one runs immediately before the controller method receives arguments?
Where would you put correlation ID creation, role authorization, DTO validation, response envelope mapping, and final error shaping?
followUps.purpose: Check whether ordering knowledge translates into design choices.

en.title: NestJS request-response lifecycle order
en.displayQuestion: What is the full execution order in NestJS from request in to response out?
en.displayIntent: Assess exact lifecycle ordering across middleware, guards, interceptors, pipes, handlers, and filters.
guidance: Walk the request from middleware to guards, interceptors, pipes, controller, service, interceptor return path, filters on error, and final response.
Mention global, controller, route, and parameter scopes where they matter.
Explain what changes when an exception is thrown before the handler returns.
commonMistakes: Putting validation before guard decisions.
Forgetting that interceptors wrap both before and after handler execution.
Treating exception filters as a normal success-response step.

vi.title: Thứ tự request-response lifecycle trong NestJS
vi.displayQuestion: Thứ tự thực thi đầy đủ trong NestJS từ lúc request đi vào tới lúc response trả ra là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá khả năng nắm chính xác thứ tự middleware, guard, interceptor, pipe, handler và filter.
guidance: Đi theo request từ middleware, guards, interceptors, pipes, controller, service, interceptor ở chiều trả về, filters khi có lỗi, rồi tới response.
Nhắc tới scope global, controller, route và parameter khi phù hợp.
Giải thích điều gì thay đổi khi có exception trước khi handler trả kết quả.
commonMistakes: Đặt validation trước guard mà không có lý do đặc biệt.
Quên interceptor có cả phần trước và sau handler.
Xem exception filter như một bước luôn chạy cho response thành công.

ja.title: NestJS の request-response lifecycle 順序
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS で request が入り response が返るまでの完全な実行順序は何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: middleware、guard、interceptor、pipe、handler、filter の正確な実行順序を評価する。
guidance: middleware、guards、interceptors、pipes、controller、service、interceptor の戻り処理、エラー時の filters、最終 response まで順に説明する。
必要に応じて global、controller、route、parameter の scope に触れる。
handler が返る前に exception が発生した場合の流れを説明する。
commonMistakes: guard 判定より前に validation を置いてしまう。
interceptor が handler の前後を包むことを忘れる。
exception filter が成功 response でも常に実行されると考える。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Request lifecycle
sourceReferences.note: Used for official middleware, guard, interceptor, pipe, filter, and response order.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands NestJS middleware and can compare it with Express middleware without flattening Nest-specific DI, module, and lifecycle behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is Middleware in NestJS and how is it similar to or different from Express middleware?

expectedSignals.label: Defines middleware as code that runs before route handlers and can inspect or modify request and response objects.

requirements.description: Defines middleware as code that runs before route handlers in the request pipeline.
Mentions inspecting or modifying request/response objects.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions the familiar Express-style pattern of req, res, next and the need to either call next or end the response.

requirements.description: Mentions the req, res, next middleware signature.
States that middleware must call next() or end the response on each path.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that Nest middleware can be function-based or class-based and class middleware can use dependency injection.

requirements.description: Mentions both function-based and class-based Nest middleware.
Explains that class middleware can use Nest dependency injection.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes middleware from Nest guards because middleware does not naturally know the target controller method or route metadata.

requirements.description: Explains that middleware usually does not naturally know the target handler or its metadata.
Contrasts guards as having ExecutionContext access to handler/class metadata.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions middleware is useful for low-level cross-cutting concerns such as logging, request IDs, body parsing, cookie parsing, or simple request enrichment.

requirements.description: Names low-level concerns such as logging, request IDs, body parsing, cookies, or request enrichment.
Avoids placing metadata-driven role or permission checks in middleware by default.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that Nest can run on different HTTP adapters, but the common middleware model is close to Express when using the Express platform.

requirements.description: Recognizes that Nest can run on different HTTP adapters such as Express or Fastify.
Explains that Express-style middleware similarity is most direct when using the Express platform adapter.

scoringHints.description: Compares Nest and Express middleware accurately, including next semantics, DI, module configuration, and limitations around route metadata.
Defines middleware correctly and compares it to Express, but misses a Nest-specific nuance such as DI or metadata limitations.
Can say middleware runs before handlers but cannot explain when to prefer it over guards or interceptors.
Answer does not show practical middleware lifecycle knowledge.

followUps.question: Would you put request ID creation, JWT verification, and role permission checks all in middleware? Why or why not?
What extra capability does class-based Nest middleware get compared with a plain function?

en.title: NestJS middleware versus Express middleware
en.displayQuestion: What is Middleware in NestJS and how is it similar to or different from Express middleware?
en.displayIntent: Check middleware fundamentals and Nest-specific differences.
guidance: Define middleware as pre-handler request/response logic using next-style flow.
Compare Express familiarity with Nest module configuration and class-based DI.
Explain why route metadata-sensitive authorization usually belongs in guards.
commonMistakes: Using middleware as a catch-all for auth, validation, and response mapping.
Ignoring next() or response termination.
Missing the difference between request-level and handler-aware components.

vi.title: Middleware NestJS so với Express middleware
vi.displayQuestion: Middleware trong NestJS là gì, và nó giống hoặc khác Express middleware như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra nền tảng middleware và các điểm khác biệt riêng của NestJS.
guidance: Định nghĩa middleware là logic chạy trước handler, xử lý request/response theo kiểu next().
So sánh sự quen thuộc với Express cùng cách Nest cấu hình qua module và hỗ trợ DI cho class middleware.
Giải thích vì sao authorization cần metadata của route thường nên nằm ở guard.
commonMistakes: Dùng middleware như nơi chứa mọi thứ: auth, validation, response mapping.
Bỏ quên next() hoặc không kết thúc response.
Không phân biệt component chỉ biết request với component biết handler.

ja.title: NestJS middleware と Express middleware の比較
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の Middleware とは何で、Express middleware とどこが似ていてどこが違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: middleware の基礎と NestJS 固有の違いを確認する。
guidance: middleware を handler 前に動く request/response ロジックとして定義する。
Express に近い next() の流れと、Nest の module 設定や class middleware の DI を比較する。
route metadata に依存する authorization は guard に置く理由を説明する。
commonMistakes: middleware を auth、validation、response mapping の万能置き場にする。
next() または response 終了を忘れる。
request だけを見る component と handler を認識する component の違いを見落とす。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware behavior, class middleware, functional middleware, and applying middleware.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Request lifecycle
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware execution position and ordering.

intent: Check whether the candidate can apply NestJS middleware correctly using functional middleware, class-based middleware, NestModule.configure, MiddlewareConsumer, forRoutes, and excludes.
primaryQuestion: How do you apply middleware in NestJS, including functional versus class-based middleware?

expectedSignals.label: Describes class-based middleware as an injectable class with a use(req, res, next) method, commonly implementing NestMiddleware.

requirements.description: Describes class middleware as a class with use(req, res, next).
Mentions NestMiddleware and the ability to use Injectable/DI.

expectedSignals.label: Describes functional middleware as a plain function receiving req, res, and next.

requirements.description: Describes functional middleware as a plain function.
Mentions req, res, and next arguments.

expectedSignals.label: Explains module-based registration with a module implementing NestModule and configure(consumer: MiddlewareConsumer).

requirements.description: Mentions implementing NestModule on a module.
Mentions configure(consumer: MiddlewareConsumer) for registration.

expectedSignals.label: Uses consumer.apply(LoggerMiddleware).forRoutes(...) and can mention route path, controller class, RequestMethod, and exclude(...).

requirements.description: Uses consumer.apply(...).forRoutes(...) to bind middleware.
Mentions route path, controller class, RequestMethod, or exclude(...).

expectedSignals.label: Mentions app.use(...) for globally bound middleware when appropriate.

requirements.description: Mentions app.use(...) for globally bound middleware.
Distinguishes app-wide registration from module/route-scoped MiddlewareConsumer registration.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that class middleware is preferred when dependencies, configuration, or testing through DI matter.

requirements.description: Explains class middleware is useful when dependencies or configuration are needed.
Mentions testability, reuse, or maintainability benefits from DI-backed class middleware.

scoringHints.description: Shows both styles, correct module registration, route scoping, DI implications, and concrete examples of forRoutes and exclude.
Can register middleware with MiddlewareConsumer but misses either functional middleware or exclude/method scoping.
Knows middleware exists but cannot write the Nest-specific registration path.
Answer is Express-only and does not demonstrate NestJS application.

followUps.question: What would the AppModule code look like to apply LoggerMiddleware only to GET /users?
When would you choose a class-based middleware over a function middleware?
How would you exclude /health from a globally applied logging middleware?

en.title: Applying functional and class middleware
en.displayQuestion: How do you apply middleware in NestJS, including functional versus class-based middleware?
en.displayIntent: Check practical middleware registration and scoping.
guidance: Explain function middleware and class middleware with use(req, res, next).
Register middleware in configure(consumer) on a NestModule using MiddlewareConsumer.
Mention forRoutes, RequestMethod, exclude, app.use, and when DI matters.
commonMistakes: Putting middleware only in providers and expecting automatic execution.
Applying expensive middleware to all routes accidentally.
Choosing function middleware even when injected services are needed.

vi.title: Áp dụng middleware dạng function và class
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn apply middleware trong NestJS như thế nào, gồm functional middleware và class-based middleware?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra khả năng đăng ký và scope middleware trong thực tế.
guidance: Giải thích function middleware và class middleware với use(req, res, next).
Đăng ký middleware trong configure(consumer) của một NestModule bằng MiddlewareConsumer.
Nhắc tới forRoutes, RequestMethod, exclude, app.use và khi nào DI quan trọng.
commonMistakes: Chỉ đưa middleware vào providers rồi nghĩ nó tự chạy.
Vô tình apply middleware nặng cho toàn bộ route.
Chọn function middleware dù cần inject service.

ja.title: function middleware と class middleware の適用
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS で functional middleware と class-based middleware をどのように適用しますか。
ja.displayIntent: middleware の実践的な登録方法と scope を確認する。
guidance: function middleware と use(req, res, next) を持つ class middleware を説明する。
NestModule の configure(consumer) で MiddlewareConsumer を使って登録する。
forRoutes、RequestMethod、exclude、app.use、DI が必要な場合に触れる。
commonMistakes: middleware を providers に置くだけで実行されると思う。
重い middleware を誤って全 route に適用する。
service injection が必要なのに function middleware を選ぶ。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for functional middleware, class middleware, NestModule.configure, and MiddlewareConsumer.

intent: Verify that the candidate knows the Guard interface contract, the canActivate signature, return types, and how Nest interprets guard decisions.
primaryQuestion: What interface does a Guard implement and what does canActivate() return?

expectedSignals.label: Names the CanActivate interface from @nestjs/common.

requirements.description: Names CanActivate as the guard interface.
Mentions importing it from @nestjs/common.

expectedSignals.label: States that guards implement canActivate(context: ExecutionContext).

requirements.description: States that guards implement canActivate(context: ExecutionContext).
Identifies ExecutionContext as the argument used to access request and metadata context.

expectedSignals.label: States that canActivate returns boolean, Promise<boolean>, or Observable<boolean>.

requirements.description: Mentions boolean return for synchronous guards.
Mentions Promise<boolean> or Observable<boolean> for async guards.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that true allows the request to continue and false denies it, typically resulting in a forbidden response.

requirements.description: Explains that returning true allows the request to continue.
Explains that returning false denies the request, typically as forbidden.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that guards can throw explicit exceptions such as UnauthorizedException or ForbiddenException to control the error semantics.

requirements.description: Mentions throwing UnauthorizedException, ForbiddenException, or another explicit Nest exception.
Explains that throwing clarifies unauthenticated versus forbidden semantics.

expectedSignals.label: Shows how to access the HTTP request through context.switchToHttp().getRequest() when needed.

requirements.description: Uses context.switchToHttp().getRequest().
Connects request access to reading headers, user data, or auth credentials.

scoringHints.description: Gives the exact interface, method signature, sync and async return options, request access, and exception behavior.
Knows CanActivate and boolean return but misses Observable, ExecutionContext, or explicit exception nuance.
Understands guards are for access checks but cannot describe the interface contract.
Answer does not demonstrate NestJS guard implementation knowledge.

followUps.question: What exact TypeScript return type would you write for an async JWT guard?
How would the guard read the Authorization header from the HTTP request?
When would you return false versus throw UnauthorizedException?
followUps.purpose: Check HTTP semantics and error clarity.

en.title: CanActivate guard contract
en.displayQuestion: What interface does a Guard implement and what does canActivate() return?
en.displayIntent: Check the NestJS guard interface and decision contract.
guidance: Name CanActivate and canActivate(context: ExecutionContext).
State boolean, Promise<boolean>, and Observable<boolean> return options.
Explain true, false, and thrown exceptions.
commonMistakes: Returning a user object instead of a boolean decision.
Ignoring async guard support.
Using the wrong lifecycle interface such as PipeTransform.

vi.title: Contract CanActivate của guard
vi.displayQuestion: Guard implement interface nào và canActivate() trả về gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra interface và contract ra quyết định của NestJS guard.
guidance: Nêu CanActivate và canActivate(context: ExecutionContext).
Nêu các kiểu trả về boolean, Promise<boolean> và Observable<boolean>.
Giải thích true, false và trường hợp throw exception.
commonMistakes: Trả về user object thay vì quyết định boolean.
Bỏ qua việc guard có thể chạy async.
Nhầm sang lifecycle interface khác như PipeTransform.

ja.title: CanActivate guard contract
ja.displayQuestion: Guard はどの interface を実装し、canActivate() は何を返しますか。
ja.displayIntent: NestJS guard の interface と判定 contract を確認する。
guidance: CanActivate と canActivate(context: ExecutionContext) を挙げる。
boolean、Promise<boolean>、Observable<boolean> の return option を説明する。
true、false、exception を throw する場合を説明する。
commonMistakes: boolean 判定ではなく user object を返す。
async guard のサポートを見落とす。
PipeTransform など別 lifecycle interface と混同する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Guards
sourceReferences.note: Used for CanActivate, ExecutionContext, return type, and guard decision behavior.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can choose between middleware and guards for authentication and authorization responsibilities in a NestJS application.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between a Guard and Middleware for authentication checks?

expectedSignals.label: Explains middleware runs earlier and is useful for low-level request preprocessing, logging, request IDs, cookie parsing, or attaching preliminary data.

requirements.description: Explains that middleware runs earlier in the request lifecycle.
Uses examples such as logging, request IDs, cookie parsing, or preliminary enrichment.

expectedSignals.label: Explains guards are intended for deciding whether a specific route handler may execute.

requirements.description: Explains that guards decide whether a specific handler may run.
Connects guards to authentication/authorization decisions rather than generic preprocessing.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions guards have ExecutionContext and can inspect controller, handler, and route metadata such as roles or public-route flags.

requirements.description: Mentions guards receive ExecutionContext.
Mentions inspecting controller/handler metadata such as roles or public-route flags.

expectedSignals.label: Separates authentication identity extraction from authorization policy checks when useful, while still recognizing many Nest apps implement JWT verification directly in a guard.

requirements.description: Distinguishes extracting/verifying identity from checking route-specific permissions.
Acknowledges that many Nest apps verify JWTs directly in a guard.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that middleware is not naturally handler-aware and can be awkward for per-route role rules.

requirements.description: States that middleware does not naturally know the target handler.
Explains why per-route role or metadata rules are awkward in middleware.

expectedSignals.label: Uses explicit HTTP exceptions for auth failure semantics instead of vague boolean or ad hoc responses.

requirements.description: Uses explicit UnauthorizedException or ForbiddenException for auth failures.
Avoids vague booleans or manually shaped ad hoc responses for auth semantics.

scoringHints.description: Draws a clear responsibility boundary, considers metadata-driven authz, and gives a pragmatic design for JWT and role checks.
Prefers guards for protected routes but misses a nuance such as request enrichment in middleware or public metadata.
Knows both terms but cannot choose a location based on lifecycle and metadata needs.
Answer is generic and not grounded in NestJS auth flow.

followUps.question: If you only need to attach a correlation ID, would you use middleware or guard? If you need @Roles metadata, which one and why?
How would your design differ between checking that a JWT exists and checking that the user can update this exact resource?

en.title: Guard versus middleware for auth
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between a Guard and Middleware for authentication checks?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate where authentication and authorization concerns should live in NestJS.
guidance: Compare early request preprocessing with handler-aware route access decisions.
Use ExecutionContext and metadata as the key guard advantage.
Discuss JWT extraction, request enrichment, roles, public routes, and exceptions.
commonMistakes: Copying Express auth middleware patterns without Nest route metadata.
Putting per-route authorization in controllers repeatedly.
Using middleware for policies that depend on handler or class decorators.

vi.title: Guard so với middleware cho auth
vi.displayQuestion: Guard và Middleware khác nhau thế nào khi dùng cho authentication check?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá nên đặt authentication và authorization ở đâu trong NestJS.
guidance: So sánh preprocessing sớm trên request với quyết định access có biết handler.
Dùng ExecutionContext và metadata như lợi thế chính của guard.
Bàn về JWT extraction, enrich request, roles, public routes và exception.
commonMistakes: Copy pattern Express auth middleware mà bỏ qua route metadata của Nest.
Lặp authorization theo từng controller.
Dùng middleware cho policy phụ thuộc decorator ở handler hoặc class.

ja.title: auth における guard と middleware の違い
ja.displayQuestion: authentication check において Guard と Middleware は何が違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: NestJS で authentication と authorization をどこに置くべきか評価する。
guidance: 早い段階の request preprocessing と handler-aware な access decision を比較する。
ExecutionContext と metadata が guard の大きな利点であることを説明する。
JWT extraction、request enrichment、roles、public routes、exception を議論する。
commonMistakes: Express の auth middleware pattern をそのままコピーし Nest の route metadata を無視する。
controller ごとに authorization を繰り返す。
handler や class decorator に依存する policy を middleware に置く。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Middleware
sourceReferences.note: Used for middleware request flow and applying middleware.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Guards
sourceReferences.note: Used for guard access-control purpose and ExecutionContext.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Authentication
sourceReferences.note: Used for global auth guard and public route metadata pattern.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands ExecutionContext as the handler-aware context object that gives guards and interceptors access to transport arguments, class, handler, and metadata.
primaryQuestion: What is ExecutionContext and why does it make Guards more powerful than Middleware?

expectedSignals.label: Defines ExecutionContext as an object passed to guards and interceptors that extends ArgumentsHost.

requirements.description: Defines ExecutionContext as the context object passed to guards and interceptors.
Mentions that ExecutionContext extends ArgumentsHost.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions transport switching helpers such as switchToHttp(), switchToWs(), and switchToRpc().

requirements.description: Mentions switchToHttp() for HTTP request/response arguments.
Mentions switchToWs(), switchToRpc(), or other transport-specific context handling.

expectedSignals.label: Explains getHandler() and getClass() expose the current route handler and controller class.

requirements.description: Explains that getHandler() exposes the current route handler method.
Explains that getClass() exposes the controller class.

expectedSignals.label: Connects getHandler/getClass with Reflector for reading metadata such as @Roles or @Public.

requirements.description: Explains using Reflector with getHandler() and getClass().
Mentions metadata such as @Roles or @Public.

expectedSignals.label: Explains this makes guards handler-aware while middleware usually only sees req, res, and next.

requirements.description: Explains that ExecutionContext makes guards aware of the target handler/class.
Contrasts middleware as usually seeing only req, res, and next.

expectedSignals.label: Can describe how the same concept supports HTTP, WebSockets, microservices, and GraphQL with adapted context handling.

requirements.description: Explains how the same authorization concept can apply across HTTP, WebSockets, microservices, or GraphQL.
Mentions adapting how request/user/context is retrieved per transport.

scoringHints.description: Explains transport arguments, handler/class access, Reflector metadata, and why this changes guard design compared with middleware.
Understands request access and metadata but misses cross-transport or Reflector details.
Knows the name ExecutionContext but cannot connect it to practical guard power.
Answer is a vague request-object explanation.

followUps.question: How would a guard read @Public metadata from both the handler and controller class?
What does context.switchToHttp().getRequest() give you, and what does context.getHandler() give you?
followUps.purpose: Separate transport args from handler metadata.

followUps.question: Why is ExecutionContext useful if the same authorization concept must work for REST and GraphQL?

en.title: ExecutionContext and handler metadata
en.displayQuestion: What is ExecutionContext and why does it make Guards more powerful than Middleware?
en.displayIntent: Check context, metadata, and transport-aware guard design.
guidance: Explain ExecutionContext as extending ArgumentsHost.
Mention switchToHttp, getHandler, getClass, and Reflector.
Compare handler-aware guards with request-only middleware.
commonMistakes: Reducing ExecutionContext to the Express request.
Missing metadata lookup through handler and class.
Ignoring WebSocket, RPC, or GraphQL adaptations.

vi.title: ExecutionContext và metadata của handler
vi.displayQuestion: ExecutionContext là gì và vì sao nó làm Guard mạnh hơn Middleware?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra context, metadata và thiết kế guard theo transport.
guidance: Giải thích ExecutionContext là phần mở rộng của ArgumentsHost.
Nhắc tới switchToHttp, getHandler, getClass và Reflector.
So sánh guard biết handler với middleware chủ yếu chỉ biết request.
commonMistakes: Thu gọn ExecutionContext thành Express request.
Không biết lookup metadata qua handler và class.
Bỏ qua WebSocket, RPC hoặc GraphQL adaptation.

ja.title: ExecutionContext と handler metadata
ja.displayQuestion: ExecutionContext とは何で、なぜ Guard を Middleware より強力にしますか。
ja.displayIntent: context、metadata、transport-aware な guard 設計を確認する。
guidance: ExecutionContext が ArgumentsHost を拡張することを説明する。
switchToHttp、getHandler、getClass、Reflector に触れる。
handler を認識する guard と request 中心の middleware を比較する。
commonMistakes: ExecutionContext を Express request と同一視する。
handler と class からの metadata lookup を見落とす。
WebSocket、RPC、GraphQL の adaptation を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Execution context
sourceReferences.note: Used for ArgumentsHost extension, handler/class access, metadata, and Reflector usage.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Guards
sourceReferences.note: Used for guard canActivate context behavior.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands the two main uses of NestJS pipes: transformation and validation, and can place pipes correctly in the lifecycle.
primaryQuestion: What are the two main use cases for Pipes: transformation and validation?

expectedSignals.label: Names transformation and validation as the two primary pipe use cases.

requirements.description: Names transformation as a primary pipe use case.
Names validation as a primary pipe use case.

expectedSignals.label: Explains transformation as converting input values into desired types or shapes, such as string route params to numbers or plain payloads to DTO instances.

requirements.description: Explains transformation as converting input values to expected types or shapes.
Uses examples such as string route params to numbers or plain payloads to DTO instances.

expectedSignals.label: Explains validation as checking incoming data and throwing an exception before the controller method runs.

requirements.description: Explains validation as checking incoming data before the controller method.
Mentions throwing an exception when validation fails.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions pipes implement PipeTransform and define transform(value, metadata).

requirements.description: Mentions PipeTransform as the pipe interface.
Mentions transform(value, metadata).

expectedSignals.label: Mentions metadata describes argument source such as body, query, param, or custom and may include metatype and data.

requirements.description: Mentions that metadata describes source such as body, query, param, or custom.
Mentions metatype and/or data in ArgumentMetadata.

expectedSignals.label: Can name examples such as ParseIntPipe, ParseBoolPipe, ParseArrayPipe, and ValidationPipe.

requirements.description: Names parse pipes such as ParseIntPipe, ParseBoolPipe, or ParseArrayPipe.
Names ValidationPipe as a common built-in pipe.

scoringHints.description: Explains transformation and validation with lifecycle position, PipeTransform contract, ArgumentMetadata, and concrete built-in pipe examples.
Covers transformation and validation but misses metadata or built-in pipe examples.
Knows ValidationPipe only and cannot explain general pipe mechanics.
Answer confuses pipes with guards, middleware, or interceptors.

followUps.question: What should happen if @Param('id', ParseIntPipe) receives abc?
followUps.purpose: Check concrete validation and transformation behavior.

followUps.question: What does ArgumentMetadata tell a custom pipe?

en.title: Pipe transformation and validation
en.displayQuestion: What are the two main use cases for Pipes: transformation and validation?
en.displayIntent: Check pipe purpose, lifecycle position, and implementation contract.
guidance: Define transformation and validation separately.
Mention PipeTransform, transform(value, metadata), and ArgumentMetadata.
Use examples like ParseIntPipe and ValidationPipe.
commonMistakes: Using pipes for authz decisions.
Assuming TypeScript types validate runtime input by themselves.
Forgetting that pipes run before the handler receives arguments.

vi.title: Transformation và validation bằng Pipe
vi.displayQuestion: Hai use case chính của Pipes là transformation và validation là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra mục đích của pipe, vị trí lifecycle và contract implement.
guidance: Định nghĩa riêng transformation và validation.
Nhắc tới PipeTransform, transform(value, metadata) và ArgumentMetadata.
Dùng ví dụ ParseIntPipe và ValidationPipe.
commonMistakes: Dùng pipe cho authorization decision.
Nghĩ TypeScript type tự validate runtime input.
Quên pipe chạy trước khi handler nhận argument.

ja.title: Pipe による transformation と validation
ja.displayQuestion: Pipes の主な 2 つの use case である transformation と validation とは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: pipe の目的、lifecycle 上の位置、実装 contract を確認する。
guidance: transformation と validation を別々に定義する。
PipeTransform、transform(value, metadata)、ArgumentMetadata に触れる。
ParseIntPipe や ValidationPipe の例を使う。
commonMistakes: pipe を authorization decision に使う。
TypeScript type だけで runtime input が validation されると考える。
pipe が handler に arguments が渡る前に動くことを忘れる。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Pipes
sourceReferences.note: Used for transformation, validation, PipeTransform, and ArgumentMetadata.

intent: Check whether the candidate can explain ValidationPipe, DTO classes, class-validator decorators, and global registration patterns.
primaryQuestion: What is ValidationPipe and how do you register it globally?

expectedSignals.label: Defines ValidationPipe as a built-in Nest pipe for validating incoming request data against DTO classes and validation decorators.

requirements.description: Defines ValidationPipe as a built-in Nest pipe for validating incoming data.
Connects validation to DTO classes and validation decorators.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions class-validator decorators such as IsEmail, IsString, IsNotEmpty, MinLength, or IsOptional.

requirements.description: Mentions decorators such as IsEmail, IsString, or IsNotEmpty.
Mentions decorators such as MinLength, IsOptional, or similar constraints.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that DTOs should be concrete classes, not interfaces, because runtime metadata is needed.

requirements.description: States that DTOs should be concrete classes for ValidationPipe.
Explains that interfaces disappear at runtime and cannot provide validation metadata.

expectedSignals.label: Shows global registration in main.ts with app.useGlobalPipes(new ValidationPipe(...)).

requirements.description: Mentions registering the global pipe in main.ts/bootstrap.
Shows app.useGlobalPipes(new ValidationPipe(...)).

expectedSignals.label: Can mention APP_PIPE provider as an alternative global registration when dependency injection or module-level configuration matters.

requirements.description: Mentions APP_PIPE as an alternative global registration mechanism.
Explains APP_PIPE is useful for DI-friendly or module-level configuration.

expectedSignals.label: Explains that invalid input normally results in a Bad Request response before the controller method executes.

requirements.description: States that invalid input normally produces a Bad Request response.
Explains that validation failure happens before the controller method runs.

scoringHints.description: Explains DTO classes, decorators, global setup, APP_PIPE alternative, error response, and runtime metadata limitations.
Covers global useGlobalPipes and DTO decorators but misses APP_PIPE or class/interface nuance.
Knows the name ValidationPipe but cannot register or configure it correctly.
Answer does not show NestJS validation practice.

followUps.question: What would a CreateUserDto contain for email and password validation?
followUps.purpose: Force concrete DTO and decorator usage.

followUps.question: Where exactly in bootstrap would you put app.useGlobalPipes(new ValidationPipe())?

en.title: Global ValidationPipe registration

guidance: Define ValidationPipe and class-validator DTO classes.
Show app.useGlobalPipes(new ValidationPipe()) in bootstrap.
Mention concrete classes, runtime metadata, and optional APP_PIPE setup.
commonMistakes: Using interfaces for runtime validation.
Assuming ValidationPipe handles business rules or persistence constraints.
Forgetting that validation happens before controller execution.

vi.title: Đăng ký ValidationPipe global

Cho ví dụ app.useGlobalPipes(new ValidationPipe()) trong bootstrap.
Nhắc tới concrete class, runtime metadata và lựa chọn APP_PIPE.
commonMistakes: Dùng interface cho runtime validation.
Nghĩ ValidationPipe xử lý luôn business rule hoặc constraint database.
Quên validation xảy ra trước controller execution.

ja.title: ValidationPipe の global 登録
ja.displayQuestion: ValidationPipe とは何で、どのように global 登録しますか。
ja.displayIntent: DTO validation と application-level pipe setup を確認する。
guidance: ValidationPipe と class-validator を使う DTO class を定義する。
bootstrap で app.useGlobalPipes(new ValidationPipe()) を示す。
concrete class、runtime metadata、APP_PIPE の選択肢に触れる。
commonMistakes: runtime validation に interface を使う。
ValidationPipe が business rule や database constraint も処理すると考える。
validation が controller 実行前に起こることを忘れる。

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands the behavioral difference between transform and whitelist in ValidationPipe and can discuss the safety trade-offs.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between transform: true and whitelist: true in ValidationPipe?

expectedSignals.label: Explains transform: true converts validated plain request payloads into DTO class instances and can convert primitive route/query values when configured.

requirements.description: Explains that transform: true converts plain payloads into DTO class instances.
Mentions primitive route/query conversion when configured.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions transform relies on class-transformer behavior and runtime metatype information.

requirements.description: Mentions class-transformer behavior.
Mentions runtime metatype information as part of transformation.

expectedSignals.label: Explains whitelist: true removes properties that do not have validation decorators on the DTO.

requirements.description: Explains whitelist: true strips properties without validation decorators.
Connects whitelist behavior to what is decorated on the DTO.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions forbidNonWhitelisted can reject unknown properties instead of silently stripping them.

requirements.description: Mentions forbidNonWhitelisted.
Explains the difference between rejecting unknown fields and silently stripping them.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes transform and whitelist solve different problems: typing/conversion versus input surface reduction.

requirements.description: Maps transform to typing/conversion behavior.
Maps whitelist to reducing accepted input surface.

expectedSignals.label: Can discuss risks such as unexpected implicit conversion, DTO properties missing decorators, and clients not noticing stripped fields.

requirements.description: Mentions risks such as unexpected implicit conversion or DTO properties missing decorators.
Mentions that clients may not notice stripped fields unless rejection or documentation is used.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates conversion from property filtering, mentions class-transformer, decorators, forbidNonWhitelisted, and operational trade-offs.
Explains both options correctly but misses one edge case such as implicit conversion or missing decorators.
Knows the options by name but cannot predict their effect on a request body.
Answer does not demonstrate practical ValidationPipe configuration knowledge.

followUps.question: If a DTO has email decorated but role has no decorator, what happens to role when whitelist is true?
When would you prefer forbidNonWhitelisted over silently stripping unknown fields?

en.title: ValidationPipe transform versus whitelist
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between transform: true and whitelist: true in ValidationPipe?
en.displayIntent: Check ValidationPipe option behavior and input-safety trade-offs.
guidance: Separate type conversion and DTO instantiation from unknown-property filtering.
Explain decorator-driven whitelist behavior and forbidNonWhitelisted.
Discuss implicit conversion and missing decorator risks.
commonMistakes: Treating transform and whitelist as synonyms.
Forgetting that whitelist depends on validation decorators.
Silently stripping important client mistakes without a deliberate API policy.

vi.title: ValidationPipe transform so với whitelist
vi.displayQuestion: transform: true và whitelist: true trong ValidationPipe khác nhau thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hành vi option của ValidationPipe và trade-off an toàn input.
guidance: Tách conversion/DTO instantiation khỏi việc lọc unknown property.
Giải thích whitelist dựa trên decorator và forbidNonWhitelisted.
Bàn về rủi ro implicit conversion và thiếu decorator.
commonMistakes: Xem transform và whitelist là đồng nghĩa.
Quên whitelist phụ thuộc validation decorators.
Âm thầm strip lỗi client quan trọng mà không có policy rõ ràng.

ja.title: ValidationPipe の transform と whitelist
ja.displayQuestion: ValidationPipe の transform: true と whitelist: true は何が違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: ValidationPipe option の挙動と input safety の trade-off を確認する。
guidance: type conversion と DTO instantiation を unknown property filtering と分けて説明する。
decorator に基づく whitelist と forbidNonWhitelisted を説明する。
implicit conversion と decorator 不足のリスクを議論する。
commonMistakes: transform と whitelist を同義と考える。
whitelist が validation decorators に依存することを忘れる。
重要な client mistake を policy なしに静かに strip する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Validation
sourceReferences.note: Used for ValidationPipe options, whitelist behavior, transform behavior, and DTO validation.

intent: Verify whether the candidate understands the ExceptionFilter interface, catch method, ArgumentsHost, and response shaping responsibilities.
primaryQuestion: What interface does an Exception Filter implement?

expectedSignals.label: Names the ExceptionFilter<T> interface from @nestjs/common.

requirements.description: Names ExceptionFilter<T> as the interface.
Mentions @nestjs/common as the source package.

expectedSignals.label: States that a filter implements catch(exception: T, host: ArgumentsHost).

requirements.description: States that a filter implements catch(exception: T, host: ArgumentsHost).
Identifies exception and ArgumentsHost as the two catch parameters.

expectedSignals.label: Explains ArgumentsHost can switch to HTTP context to access request and response.

requirements.description: Explains using host.switchToHttp() to access HTTP context.
Mentions accessing request and/or response from the HTTP context.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions filters are commonly decorated with @Catch(...) to select exception types.

requirements.description: Mentions decorating filters with @Catch(...).
Explains that @Catch selects which exception types the filter handles.

expectedSignals.label: Explains filters shape the outgoing error response and can perform safe logging.

requirements.description: Explains that filters shape the outgoing error response.
Mentions safe logging while avoiding leaking sensitive internals to clients.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions filters can be bound at method, controller, or global scope.

requirements.description: Mentions method and/or controller-level filter binding.
Mentions global filter binding for shared error envelopes.

scoringHints.description: Gives the interface, catch signature, ArgumentsHost usage, @Catch binding, scope options, and safe error-shaping purpose.
Knows ExceptionFilter and catch but misses scope or safe logging detail.

en.title: ExceptionFilter interface contract

guidance: Name ExceptionFilter<T> and catch(exception, host).
Use ArgumentsHost to access HTTP request and response.
Mention @Catch and method, controller, or global binding.
commonMistakes: Confusing filters with interceptors or pipes.
Leaking internal stack traces to clients.
Not knowing how host.switchToHttp() is used.

vi.title: Contract interface ExceptionFilter
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cơ chế implement filter và cách shape error response an toàn.
guidance: Nêu ExceptionFilter<T> và catch(exception, host).
Dùng ArgumentsHost để lấy HTTP request và response.
Nhắc tới @Catch và binding ở method, controller hoặc global.

Lộ stack trace nội bộ ra client.
Không biết dùng host.switchToHttp().

ja.title: ExceptionFilter interface contract

intent: Check whether the candidate can distinguish Nest's HttpException base class and built-in subclasses from custom domain exception classes and unknown runtime errors.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between HttpException and a custom exception class?

expectedSignals.label: Explains HttpException is Nest's base HTTP-aware exception that carries response body and HTTP status.

requirements.description: Explains HttpException as Nest's base HTTP-aware exception.
Mentions that it carries HTTP status and response body.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions built-in subclasses such as BadRequestException, UnauthorizedException, ForbiddenException, NotFoundException, and InternalServerErrorException.

requirements.description: Names built-ins such as BadRequestException, UnauthorizedException, ForbiddenException, or NotFoundException.
Mentions InternalServerErrorException or equivalent server-error subclass.

expectedSignals.label: Explains a custom exception class can extend HttpException to standardize domain-specific error codes, messages, and status selection.

requirements.description: Explains that a custom HTTP exception can extend HttpException.
Mentions standardizing domain-specific error codes, messages, and HTTP status.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes custom HttpException subclasses from arbitrary Error subclasses, which the default filter may treat as internal server errors.

requirements.description: Separates custom HttpException subclasses from arbitrary Error subclasses.
Mentions that unknown Errors may be treated as internal server errors by default.

expectedSignals.label: Discusses when to prefer built-in exceptions for simple HTTP semantics and custom exceptions for reusable domain or API error contracts.

requirements.description: Explains when built-in exceptions are sufficient for straightforward HTTP cases.
Explains when custom exceptions are justified for reusable domain or API error contracts.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions exception filters can normalize or map exceptions, but should not hide root-cause logging.

requirements.description: Mentions using filters to normalize or map exceptions.
States that filters should log root causes safely rather than hiding them.

scoringHints.description: Separates built-in HTTP exceptions, custom HttpException subclasses, plain Error behavior, and filter-based normalization with clear trade-offs.
Understands HttpException and custom subclasses but misses unknown-error default handling or error-contract design.
Knows exceptions produce HTTP errors but cannot choose between built-in and custom classes.
Answer lacks NestJS-specific exception behavior.

followUps.question: When would BadRequestException be better than creating InvalidUserInputException?
If a service throws new Error('database password invalid'), what should the client see and what should logs capture?
followUps.purpose: Check unknown error and leakage handling.

followUps.question: What fields would you include in a reusable domain exception response?

en.title: HttpException versus custom exceptions
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between HttpException and a custom exception class?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate exception class design and HTTP error contract judgment.
guidance: Explain HttpException, built-in subclasses, and custom subclasses.
Distinguish HTTP-aware exceptions from unknown plain errors.
Discuss stable API error contracts and safe logging.
commonMistakes: Throwing plain Error for expected HTTP cases.
Over-creating custom classes without reusable semantics.
Exposing internal exception details to clients.

vi.title: HttpException so với custom exception

guidance: Giải thích HttpException, các subclass built-in và custom subclass.
Phân biệt exception có HTTP semantics với plain error không biết trước.
Bàn về API error contract ổn định và logging an toàn.
commonMistakes: Throw plain Error cho các HTTP case dự kiến.
Tạo quá nhiều custom class không có semantic tái sử dụng.
Expose chi tiết exception nội bộ ra client.

ja.title: HttpException と custom exception

ja.displayIntent: exception class 設計と HTTP error contract の判断を評価する。
guidance: HttpException、built-in subclass、custom subclass を説明する。
HTTP-aware exception と未知の plain error を区別する。

commonMistakes: 想定された HTTP case で plain Error を throw する。
再利用できる意味のない custom class を作りすぎる。
内部 exception の詳細を client に公開する。

intent: Verify that the candidate understands the NestInterceptor interface, intercept method, CallHandler, next.handle(), and the role of RxJS Observables in response manipulation.

expectedSignals.label: Explains next.handle() triggers the route handler and returns an Observable representing the response stream.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can distinguish interceptors and pipes by lifecycle position, inputs, outputs, and appropriate responsibilities.

guidance: PipeTransform の value/metadata と NestInterceptor の context/CallHandler を比較する。
input validation/transformation と response stream handling を分ける。
具体的な task で適切な仕組みを選ぶ。
commonMistakes: DTO validation に interceptor を使う。
pipe が final response を shape すると期待する。
interceptor の Observable stream を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Interceptors
sourceReferences.note: Used for interceptor handler wrapping and Observable response stream.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Pipes
sourceReferences.note: Used for pipe transformation, validation, PipeTransform, and ArgumentMetadata.

intent: Check whether the candidate can implement a protected-by-default NestJS app using a global guard while allowing explicit public routes through metadata.
primaryQuestion: How do you apply a Guard globally while allowing specific routes to be public?

expectedSignals.label: Registers the auth guard globally using APP_GUARD in a module or app.useGlobalGuards when DI is not needed.

requirements.description: Mentions registering a global guard with APP_GUARD.
Mentions app.useGlobalGuards when DI is not needed.

expectedSignals.label: Explains APP_GUARD is preferred for DI-friendly global guards.

requirements.description: Explains that APP_GUARD supports dependency injection.
Connects DI-friendly global guards to dependencies such as Reflector or auth services.

expectedSignals.label: Creates a @Public decorator using SetMetadata or a typed Reflector decorator with a stable metadata key.

requirements.description: Creates or describes a @Public decorator.
Uses SetMetadata or a typed Reflector decorator with a stable metadata key.

expectedSignals.label: In canActivate, reads metadata with Reflector, commonly getAllAndOverride, against context.getHandler() and context.getClass().

requirements.description: Mentions Reflector.getAllAndOverride or equivalent metadata resolution.
Checks both context.getHandler() and context.getClass().

expectedSignals.label: Returns true early for public routes before token verification.

requirements.description: Returns true when public metadata is present.
Explains that public routes bypass token verification early.

expectedSignals.label: Keeps the default policy protected-by-default and makes public access explicit at method or controller level.

requirements.description: States that routes are protected by default under the global guard.
Explains that public access must be explicitly marked at method or controller level.

scoringHints.description: Describes APP_GUARD, @Public metadata, Reflector getAllAndOverride, handler/class lookup, DI concerns, and protected-by-default behavior.
Explains global guard and @Public decorator but misses APP_GUARD or handler/class override nuance.
Can protect routes but lacks a scalable public-route exception pattern.
Answer relies on manual per-route checks or hard-coded URLs.

followUps.question: What exact metadata key and decorator would you create for public routes?
Why use Reflector.getAllAndOverride with context.getHandler() and context.getClass()?
followUps.purpose: Check method versus controller-level metadata resolution.

en.title: Global guard with public routes
en.displayQuestion: How do you apply a Guard globally while allowing specific routes to be public?
en.displayIntent: Check protected-by-default auth design with metadata-based public exceptions.
guidance: Register the guard globally with APP_GUARD or useGlobalGuards.
Create @Public metadata and read it with Reflector.
Use handler and class metadata to allow method or controller-level public routes.
commonMistakes: Hard-coding public paths in the guard.
Missing DI implications of global guard registration.
Checking only one metadata level and breaking overrides.

vi.title: Global guard với public routes
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn apply Guard global nhưng vẫn cho phép một số route public như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra thiết kế auth protected-by-default với public exception dựa trên metadata.
guidance: Đăng ký guard global bằng APP_GUARD hoặc useGlobalGuards.
Tạo @Public metadata và đọc bằng Reflector.
Dùng metadata ở handler và class để hỗ trợ public ở method hoặc controller.
commonMistakes: Hard-code public path trong guard.
Bỏ qua ảnh hưởng DI khi đăng ký global guard.
Chỉ check một cấp metadata khiến override bị sai.

ja.title: public routes を持つ global guard
ja.displayQuestion: Guard を global に適用しつつ、一部 route を public にするにはどうしますか。
ja.displayIntent: metadata-based な public exception を持つ protected-by-default auth 設計を確認する。
guidance: APP_GUARD または useGlobalGuards で guard を global 登録する。
@Public metadata を作り Reflector で読む。
handler と class metadata を使い method または controller-level public routes を許可する。
commonMistakes: guard 内で public path を hard-code する。
global guard 登録時の DI の影響を見落とす。
metadata の片方の level だけを見て override を壊す。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Authentication
sourceReferences.note: Used for APP_GUARD global auth guard, @Public metadata, and Reflector public-route bypass.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Execution context
sourceReferences.note: Used for metadata lookup with handler and class contexts.

intent: Verify whether the candidate understands the @Catch decorator on NestJS exception filters and how it selects exception types or catch-all behavior.
primaryQuestion: What does @Catch() do in an Exception Filter?

expectedSignals.label: Explains @Catch marks a class as an exception filter and tells Nest which exception types the filter should handle.

requirements.description: Explains that @Catch marks a class as an exception filter.
Explains that @Catch tells Nest which exception types the filter handles.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions @Catch(HttpException) catches HttpException and subclasses when the filter is bound.

requirements.description: Mentions @Catch(HttpException).
Explains it catches HttpException and subclasses when the filter is bound.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions @Catch() with no arguments can be used as a catch-all filter for unhandled exception types.

requirements.description: Mentions @Catch() with no arguments.
Explains that it can catch unhandled exception types as a catch-all filter.

expectedSignals.label: Explains the decorator does not by itself make the filter global; it still must be bound with @UseFilters, app.useGlobalFilters, or APP_FILTER.

requirements.description: States that @Catch alone does not make a filter global or active everywhere.
Mentions binding with @UseFilters, app.useGlobalFilters, or APP_FILTER.

expectedSignals.label: Explains the catch(exception, host) method still performs the response shaping or logging.

requirements.description: Explains that catch(exception, host) contains the actual handling logic.
Mentions response shaping and/or logging inside the catch method.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions catch-all filters must be careful not to leak unknown internal errors.

requirements.description: Warns that catch-all filters handle unknown internal errors.
States they should not leak stack traces, secrets, or internal details to clients.

scoringHints.description: Explains exception type metadata, catch-all behavior, binding requirement, catch method responsibility, and safe unknown-error handling.
Understands @Catch selects handled exception types but misses binding or catch-all safety nuance.
Knows @Catch is related to filters but cannot predict what it catches or how it is applied.
Answer confuses decorators with runtime try/catch and lacks NestJS filter mechanics.

followUps.question: What changes if you write @Catch(HttpException) versus @Catch() with no arguments?
After writing @Catch(), what else must you do for the filter to run on routes?

en.title: @Catch decorator in exception filters
en.displayQuestion: What does @Catch() do in an Exception Filter?
en.displayIntent: Check exception filter decoration, type selection, and binding behavior.
guidance: Explain @Catch as metadata for handled exception types.
Compare @Catch(HttpException) with catch-all @Catch().
Mention binding via @UseFilters, useGlobalFilters, or APP_FILTER.
commonMistakes: Assuming @Catch alone registers the filter globally.
Confusing it with JavaScript try/catch.
Leaking unknown exception details from a catch-all filter.

vi.title: Decorator @Catch trong exception filter
vi.displayQuestion: @Catch() làm gì trong một Exception Filter?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra decorator của exception filter, cách chọn exception type và binding.
guidance: Giải thích @Catch là metadata cho các exception type filter xử lý.
So sánh @Catch(HttpException) với catch-all @Catch().
Nhắc tới binding qua @UseFilters, useGlobalFilters hoặc APP_FILTER.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ @Catch tự đăng ký filter global.
Nhầm nó với JavaScript try/catch.
Lộ chi tiết unknown exception từ catch-all filter.

ja.title: exception filter の @Catch decorator
ja.displayQuestion: Exception Filter における @Catch() は何をしますか。
ja.displayIntent: exception filter decoration、type selection、binding behavior を確認する。
guidance: @Catch を handled exception types の metadata として説明する。
@Catch(HttpException) と catch-all @Catch() を比較する。
@UseFilters、useGlobalFilters、APP_FILTER による binding に触れる。
commonMistakes: @Catch だけで filter が global 登録されると思う。
JavaScript の try/catch と混同する。
catch-all filter から unknown exception の詳細を漏らす。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Exception filters
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Catch, catch-all filters, custom filters, and binding filters.
